cue: How do you tell someone to act while the moment is right?

es.text: A hierro candente, batir de repente
es.literal: on red-hot iron, strike at once
es.meaning: Act while the moment is hot; deal with it now.
es.example: Habla con él hoy que está receptivo: a hierro candente, batir de repente.
es.exampleTranslation: Talk to him today while he's open to it — strike while the iron's hot.

it.text: Battere il ferro finché è caldo
it.literal: to beat the iron while it is hot
it.example: Parlagli adesso: bisogna battere il ferro finché è caldo.
it.exampleTranslation: Talk to him now — strike while the iron's hot.

bridge.note: The same forge, and this time Spanish is the one showing off: candente/repente rhymes, so the refrán arrives as a couplet, while Italian states the smithing plainly with a subordinate clause. The exact inversion of chi non risica non rosica, where the rhyme sits on the Italian side. Neither language rhymes on principle — each just got lucky with different proverbs.

interference.es_to_it: The Spanish rhyme cannot be carried over; Italian keeps the finché clause — battere il ferro finché è caldo.
interference.it_to_es: Translating the Italian literally ('golpear el hierro mientras está caliente') is understood but is not the refrán, and loses the rhyme that makes it memorable.

review.notes: From Joe's Italian proverb list 2026-07-18 (learner-site discovery; all glosses and examples authored fresh). ES counterpart chosen by Claude from recall — UNVERIFIED. Pairs with 0054 as a rhyme-inversion set — a themed two-day sequence.
